data.title: Bitcoin Cracks Below $90K as Wall Street Surges in a Stunning Post?Fed Split
data.description:
Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) slide is turning into one of the most intriguing divergences in global markets. While the Federal Reserve pushed through a quarter-point rate cut and expressed optimism about the US economy lifting the S&P500 by 0.7% to its strongest Fed-day gain since March Bitcoin moved the other way, falling as much as 3.2% in Asia hours and briefly breaking below $90,000. The drop came on the heels of a weeks-long selloff that began in early October when a major liquidation erased about $19 billion in levered bets, placing crypto traders on a far different footing than investors in traditional risk assets.

Through early morning in New York, Bitcoin had recovered part of the slide to trade down around 2.3% at $90,260, with Ether lower by roughly 4.3%. But the narrative forming around this market is one of hesitation rather than relief. FalconX derivatives specialist Sean McNulty described the setup as a clear decoupling, pointing to $88,500 as the next level on traders' screens and $85,000 as a possible line that could reshape sentiment if tested. Even the sudden resurgence of ETF demand $224 million of net inflows on Wednesday, including $193 million into BlackRock's iShares Bitcoin Trust, its strongest haul in 30 days wasn't enough to keep the token above $94,000. Strategy Inc.'s acquisition of 10,624 tokens worth $962.7 million between Dec. 1 and Dec. 7, its largest purchase since July, also struggled to shift the tone, suggesting the market may still be absorbing persistent structural selling.

Underneath the spot moves, derivatives markets are offering their own signals. Funding rates in perpetual futures the deepest pool of Bitcoin trading activity turned negative during Asia hours, indicating bearish traders are now paying bullish counterparts to hold short positions. And while Bitcoin recently rebounded from a capitulation low of $80,537, IG Australia analyst Tony Sycamore warned that the setup could still allow for a turn lower and for a retest and break of that level. With crypto lagging while equities gain momentum, investors are watching whether this gap narrows or becomes the defining tension heading into year-end.

data.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Unexpectedly Narrows In September
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Reflecting a sharp increase in the value of exports, the Commerce Department released a report on Thursday showing the U.S. trade deficit unexpectedly narrowed in the month of September.

The Commerce Department said the trade deficit shrank to $52.8 billion in September from a revised $59.3 billion in August.

Economists had expected the trade deficit to widen to $63.3 billion from the $59.6 billion originally reported for the previous month.

The unexpectedly narrower trade deficit came as the value of exports jumped by 3.0 percent to $289.3 billion, while the value of imports climbed by 0.6 percent to $342.1 billion.

data.title: SK On, Ford To Dissolve Battery Joint Venture In US
data.description:
DEARBORN (dpa-AFX) - South Korea-based SK On, a subsidiary of SK Innovation Co., Ltd. (096770.KS), Thursday announced its decision to dissolve its joint venture with Ford Motor Company (F) to focus on other growth sectors.

As part of this move, each company will independently own and operate its portion of the joint battery factories' production facilities in the United States.

Notably, a Ford subsidiary will take full ownership of the battery plants in Kentucky, whereas SK On will assume full ownership and operate the battery plant in Tennessee.

The battery maker expects the recent move to strengthen battery productivity as well as boost its entry into North American energy storage system business.

SK's stock closed at KRW 112,300.00, down 3.69 percent on the Korean Stock Exchange. Meanwhile, Ford's stock is trading at $13.41, up 0.02 percent in the pre-market hours on the New York Stock Exchange.

data.title: Fitch Upgrades National Bank of Oman to 'BB+'; Stable Outlook
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Dubai-11 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has upgraded National Bank of Oman SAOG's (NBO) Long-Term (LT) Issuer Default Rating (IDR) to 'BB+' from 'BB'. Fitch has also upgraded the bank's Viability Rating (VR) and Government Support Rating (GSR) to 'bb+' from 'bb'. The Outlook is Stable.

The VR and LT IDR upgrades reflect Fitch's assessment of improving operating conditions for Omani banks, as indicated by the revision of the operating environment score to 'bbb-'/stable from 'bb+' /positive. We expect these favourable conditions to benefit NBO's business and financial profiles.

The GSR upgrade reflects Fitch's view of an improved ability of the Omani authorities to support domestic banks. This follows an upgrade of Oman's sovereign rating on 8 December 2025 (see "Fitch Upgrades Oman to 'BBB-'; Outlook Stable" at www.fitchratings.com), which was driven by sustained improvements in Oman's public and external balance sheets, and Fitch's greater confidence that Oman will maintain a prudent policy in a lower oil price environment.

Key Rating Drivers

NBO's IDRs are driven by its VR and underpinned by potential support from the Omani authorities, as reflected in its GSR. The VR reflects the bank's strong domestic franchise, balanced business model, improved profitability, reasonable capitalisation, stable funding and liquidity, and recovering asset quality. It also reflects the bank's high concentration risks.

Favourable Operating Conditions: Business conditions remain favourable for Omani banks, supported by high, but moderating, oil prices (2026F: USD63/barrel, around Oman's fiscal break-even price). The authorities' commitment to diversifying the economy as part of Vision 2040 should provide growth opportunities for banks.

Universal Business Model: NBO is a medium-sized bank with a 12% market share in Oman. It has a well-balanced business model and a loan book that is focused on retail and corporate banking.

High Concentrations: The composition of NBO's loan book is similar to that of its domestic peers. Most of its lending is domestic, which constrains underwriting standards to the domestic market. Single-name and sector concentration levels are high.

Improving Asset-Quality Metrics: NBO's asset quality is continuing to recover, with the Stage 3 loans ratio at 4.5% at end-3Q25. Stage 2 loans fell to 13.8% of total loans at end-3Q25 (end-2023: 20.7%) but remain high by international standards. This is due largely to NBO's and the Central Bank of Oman's conservative loans classification guidelines. We expect Stage 3 loans to stabilise at around current levels in 2026.

Improved Profitability: Profitability metrics improved over 2023-9M25, supported by stronger revenue, lower operating expenses and moderated loan impairment charges (LICs; annualised 0.3% of average gross loans in 9M25). The bank's operating profit was an annualised 1.8% of risk-weighted assets (RWAs) in 9M25, up from 1.7% in 2023. We expect the bank's performance to remain stable, supported by reasonable asset quality, while lower interest rates will have a contained impact on margins.

Adequate Capitalisation: NBO's common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio amounted to 11% at end-3Q25, without accounting for interim profits. The CET1 ratio provided a reasonable 150bp buffer above its regulatory minimum requirement. This supports our assessment of the bank's capitalisation, despite high concentration risks. We forecast NBO to operate with a CET1 ratio of about 11.5% in 2026.

Stable Funding Profile: NBO's funding profile benefits from its stable retail franchise - about a quarter of customer deposits - and moderate deposit concentrations. However, like its peers, reliance on deposits from the government and government-related entities (GREs) remains high. Liquidity is reasonable, with a loans-to-deposits ratio of 107% at end-3Q25.

Moderate Probability of Sovereign Support: NBO's GSR is in line with the 'bb+' GSR for domestic systemically important banks, reflecting our view on the Omani authorities' high propensity to support the banking system, due to high contagion risk in the sector and the importance of the banking system in building the domestic economy. The state's financial flexibility and ability to provide support is moderate but has been improving in recent years.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

A downgrade of NBO's LT IDR is unlikely given the Stable Outlook and would require a downgrade of both the VR and the GSR.

A downgrade of the GSR could be triggered by a sovereign downgrade.

A VR downgrade could result from a material weakening of its asset-quality metrics, with the Stage 3 ratio increasing above 6%, and of its capital ratios, with the CET1 ratio falling below 11%, on a sustained basis.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of NBO's LT IDR may primarily result from a sovereign upgrade, in which case we would upgrade the GSR.

A VR upgrade is currently unlikely and conditional to a material improvement in the bank's business profile and overall financial profile.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

NBO's Short-Term IDR of 'B' is the only option mapping to a 'BB+' LT IDR.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The Short-Term IDR is sensitive to changes in the bank's LT IDR.

VR ADJUSTMENTS

The earnings and profitability score of 'bb+' is below the 'bbb' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: historical and future metrics (negative).

The funding and liquidity score of 'bb' is below the 'bbb' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: deposit structure (negative).

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3'means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. data.title: SNB On Course to Keep Rates Steady Through 2026 — Market Talk
data.description: The Swiss National Bank's widely-expected decision to keep rates at 0% came with a reinforced bias to stay on hold for the foreseeable future, BNP Paribas economist Dani Stoilova says. Discussions around the forecast changes and recent developments--such as the positives of a U.S. trade deal and negative inflation surprises--had a hawkish undertone, she says in a note. While lingering uncertainty about the global backdrop leaves risks tilted toward additional easing in the near term, a move of this sort would require sustained concerns about medium-term deflation, Stoilova says. Fading risks of deflation, and European Central Bank rate hikes should allow the SNB to hike again next, starting in the third quarter of 2027, for an end-2027 interest rate of 0.5%, she says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

data.title: Fitch Upgrades Bank Dhofar to 'BB+'; Stable Outlook
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Dubai-11 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has upgraded Bank Dhofar S.A.O.G.'s (BD) Long-Term (LT) Issuer Default Rating (IDR) to 'BB+', from 'BB', and its Government Support Rating (GSR) to 'bb+', from 'bb'. Fitch has affirmed the bank's Viability Rating (VR) at 'bb'. The Outlook is Stable.

The GSR upgrade reflects Fitch's view of an improved ability of the Omani authorities to support domestic banks. This follows an upgrade of Oman's rating on 8 December 2025 (see "Fitch Upgrades Oman to BBB-; Outlook Stable", at www.fitchratings.com), which was driven by sustained improvements in the country's public and external balance sheets, and Fitch's greater confidence that it will maintain a prudent policy in a lower oil price environment.

Key Rating Drivers

BD's LT IDR is driven by potential support from the Omani authorities, as captured by its GSR of 'bb+'. The Stable Outlook on the IDR reflects that on the sovereign. The bank's VR reflects its strong domestic franchise, and adequate capitalisation and liquidity. These factors are counterbalanced by pressure, though reducing, on the bank's asset quality, its weaker-than-average profitability and high concentration risks.

Moderate Probability of Sovereign Support: BD's GSR is in line with the 'bb+' GSR for domestic systemically important banks, reflecting the Omani authorities' high propensity to support the banking system due to the high contagion risk in the sector and the importance of the banking system in building the domestic economy. Their financial flexibility and ability to provide support is moderate but has been improving.

Favourable Operating Conditions: Business conditions remain favourable for Omani banks, supported by high, but moderating, oil prices (2026F: USD63/barrel, close to Oman's fiscal break-even price). The authorities' commitment to diversifying the economy as part of Vision 2040 should provide growth opportunities for banks. Fitch has also revised up the operating environment score for Omani banks to 'bbb-'/stable, from 'bb+'/positive.

Strong Domestic Franchise: BD had a market share of 12% in sector assets at end-3Q25. The bank has a large domestic branch network and a strong presence in retail banking. Its business profile is split between retail, corporate and government lending.

Concentrated Balance Sheet: BD's underwriting standards reflect its high lending concentrations, mirroring a small and undiversified Omani economy, which limits diversification opportunities. The composition of the bank's loan book is similar to that of domestic peers, although it is more exposed to the volatile construction industry.

Loan Quality Yet to Stabilise: The bank's impaired loans ratio decreased to 4.9% at end-3Q25 (end-2023: 5.4%), supported by sizeable write-offs and strong lending growth. BD's specific reserve coverage of impaired exposures remains modest, at 56% at end-3Q25. We expect BD's impaired-loans ratio to remain below 5% in 2025 and reduce to about 4% at end-2026, supported by the resolution of impaired credit exposures and continued loan growth.

Weaker Profitability than Peers: BD's net interest margin fell to an annualised 2.4% in 9M25, from 2.6% in 2023, due to increased funding costs since 2023, although this is has now started to reduce. Nonetheless, the strong generation of net fees and commissions income and reasonable loan-impairment charges supported a slightly higher operating profit of 1.3% of risk-weighted assets in 9M25 (2024: 1.2%; 2023: 1.1%), but this remains lower than at peers. We expect the bank's performance to remain constrained by provisioning needs in 2026 and forecast an operating profit of no higher than 1.4% of risk-weighted assets in 2026.

Adequate Capital Buffers: BD's common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio fell to 12.1% at end-3Q25, from 13% in 2023, but still provided an adequate, 260bp buffer above the regulatory minimum requirement. This supports our assessment of the bank's capitalisation, which captures its high concentration risks. The total capital ratio (end-3Q25: 16.8%) is supported by additional Tier 1 (AT1) perpetual bonds (3.6% of risk-weighted assets) and a subordinated loan (0.7% of risk-weighted assets). We expect the CET1 ratio to be 12%-12.5% in 2026.

Adequate Funding and Liquidity: BD is mainly funded by stable customer deposits, which accounted for 86% of non-equity funding at end-3Q25. However, as at peers, a high share of deposits from government-related entities results in funding concentrations. Liquidity is adequate, with the liquidity coverage and net stable funding (104%) ratios over the regulatory minimum of 100%, at 126% and 104%, respectively, at end-3Q25.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

A downgrade of BD's LT IDR would require a downgrade of the GSR, which may be triggered by a sovereign downgrade.

A VR downgrade could result from a material and persistent weakening of its asset-quality metrics, with the Stage 3 ratio increasing above 8%, and lower capital ratios, with the CET1 ratio falling below 11%, on a sustained basis.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of BD's LT IDR and GSR may result from a sovereign upgrade.

An upgrade of the VR would require a sustained improvement in the bank's asset quality and profitability.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

BD's Short-Term IDR of 'B' is the only option mapping to a 'BB+' LT IDR.

BD's LT IDR (xgs) is at the level of the VR. The Short-Term IDR (xgs) is mapped to the bank's LT IDR (xgs).

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The Short-Term IDR is sensitive to changes in the bank's LT IDR.

BD's LT IDR (xgs) would mirror changes to its VR. The Short-Term IDR (xgs) is sensitive to changes to the LT IDR (xgs).

VR ADJUSTMENTS

The funding and liquidity score of 'bb' is below the 'bbb' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: deposit structure (negative).

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Public Ratings with Credit Linkage to other ratings

The IDRs of BD are linked to Oman's sovereign ratings.

ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3'means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visithttps://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: More than 12 million face acute hunger in Myanmar, WFP says
data.description:
More than 12 million people in Myanmar will face acute hunger next year as mounting violence forces more people to flee their homes, the United Nations World Food Programme said on Thursday.

Around 1 million of those will face emergency levels of hunger, meaning they will need lifesaving support, the agency added.

“Conflict and deprivation are converging to strip away people's basic means of survival, yet the world isn’t paying attention,” Michael Dunford, WFP Country Director in Myanmar, said in a statement.

"This is one of the worst hunger crises on the planet, and one of the least funded."

A spokesman for Myanmar's ruling junta did not respond to calls seeking comment on WFP's projections.

Myanmar has been in crisis since the military seized power in 2021 and cracked down on protests, prompting a nationwide armed uprising.

At the moment, more than 400,000 children and mothers with acute malnutrition are surviving on plain rice or watery porridge, the WFP said.

Myanmar's military has previously detained food-security researchers and warned aid workers not to release information revealing that millions of people are experiencing severe hunger, Reuters has reported.

The military is planning to hold elections starting on Dec. 28. Critics and human rights groups have dismissed the vote as an attempt by ruling generals to legitimise their rule through proxy political parties - a charge dismissed by the junta.

An estimated 3.6 million people have been displaced within Myanmar, with scores fleeing their homes multiple times and often ending up in makeshift shelters with limited access to food, healthcare, and clean water, the U.N. says.

Nearly a third of Myanmar's population of 51.3 million people will need humanitarian assistance next year, according to a U.N. assessment released on Wednesday.

"Conflict is expected to intensify around the elections, while climate shocks and economic decline will continue to erode resilience," it said.

data.title: Fitch Upgrades Sohar International Bank to 'BB+'; Stable Outlook
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Dubai-11 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has upgraded Sohar International Bank SAOG's (SIB) Long-Term (LT) Issuer Default Rating (IDR) to 'BB+' from 'BB', and its Viability Rating (VR) and Government Support Rating (GSR) to 'bb+' from 'bb'.

The VR and LT IDR upgrades reflect Fitch's assessment of improving operating conditions for Omani banks, as indicated by the revision of the operating environment score to 'bbb-'/stable from 'bb+' /positive. We expect these favourable conditions to benefit SIB's business and financial profiles.

The GSR upgrade reflects Fitch's view of the Omani authorities' improved ability to support domestic banks. This follows an upgrade of Oman's sovereign rating on 8 December 2025 (see "Fitch Upgrades Oman to 'BBB-'; Outlook Stable" at www.fitchratings.com), which was driven by sustained improvements in Oman's public and external balance sheets, and Fitch's greater confidence that Oman will maintain a prudent policy in a lower oil price environment.

Key Rating Drivers

SIB's IDRs are driven by its VR and underpinned by potential support from the Omani authorities, as reflected in its GSR. The VR captures the bank's improved business profile since 2023, following its merger with HSBC Bank Oman SAOG (HBON), its larger deposit franchise, adequate capitalisation and stabilising asset quality.

Favourable Operating Conditions: Business conditions remain favourable for Omani banks, supported by high, but moderating, oil prices (2026F: USD63/barrel, around Oman's fiscal break-even price). The authorities' commitment to diversifying the economy as part of Vision 2040 should provide growth opportunities for banks.

Strengthened Business Profile after Merger: SIB's franchise has strengthened considerably after the merger, becoming Oman's second-largest bank, with a share of 18% by total assets at end-3Q25. This supports the bank's pricing power, leading to a reduction in funding costs, and a stronger footprint in lower-risk segments, in which HBON has a well-established presence.

Reasonable Risk Profile: SIB's sector loan diversification has improved following the merger, and its exposure to the construction and real estate sectors was contained at 11% of gross loans at end-3Q25. Single-borrower loan concentration was also lower than at peers in 9M25. SIB's loans continued to grow rapidly, at 18.1% in 9M25, versus 10% in 2024, but down from 37% (due to the merger with HBON) in 2023. We expect loan growth of about 10% in 2026, above that of the sector, although it could be higher depending on market conditions.

Reducing Impaired Loan Ratio: SIB's Stage 3 loans ratio declined to 4.6% at end-3Q25 from 4.8% at end-2023, although it remains above the peer average of 4.2%. Reserve coverage of impaired loans remained strong at 153%, which was driven by the bank's decision to boost provisioning since 2023 after recognising a sizeable one-off bargain purchase gain from the HBON acquisition. We expect the impaired loans ratio to be stable in 2026.

Sound Profitability: SIB's net interest margin (NIM) tightened to an annualised 2.3% in 9M25, from 2.7% in 2023, due mostly to a lower asset yield. Lower loan impairment charges (LICs) and a reasonable operating efficiency supported the bank's adequate operating profit at 2% of risk-weighted assets (RWAs) in 9M25, although it was down from 2.3% in 2024 due to the tighter NIM and higher operating expenses. We expect this metric to remain around 2% in 2026.

Adequate Capitalisation: SIB's common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio fell to 14.2% at end-3Q25 (end-2024: 16.4%%) on high asset growth, but remains adequate. Fitch expects the ratio to remain about 14.5% in 2026, with the bank's sound profitability balancing rapid growth.

Expanded Deposit Franchise: SIB's reliance on non-deposit funding fell to a moderate 11% of non-equity funding at end-3Q25, from 26% at end-2022, on rapid deposit growth in 2023-9M25. The loans/deposits ratio increased to 84% at end-3Q25 (end-2024: 80%), due to lending growth outpacing that of deposits, but remains at the lower end of the Omani banking sector.

Moderate Probability of Sovereign Support: SIB's GSR is in line with the 'bb+' GSR for domestic systemically important banks, reflecting our view on the Omani authorities' high propensity to support the banking system due to the high contagion risk in the sector and the importance of the banking system in building the domestic economy. The state's financial flexibility and ability to provide support is moderate but has been improving in recent years.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

A downgrade of SIB's LT IDR is unlikely given the Stable Outlook and would require a downgrade of both the VR and the GSR.

A downgrade of the GSR would be triggered by a sovereign downgrade.

A VR downgrade could result from a material weakening in its asset-quality metrics, with the Stage 3 ratio increasing above 6%, and of its capital ratios, with the CET1 ratio falling below 11%, on a sustained basis.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade of SIB's LT IDR may primarily result from a sovereign upgrade, in which case we would also upgrade the bank's GSR.

An upgrade of the VR is currently unlikely, unless the bank improves its business profile, in particular by significantly increasing its market share, and further strengthening its overall financial profile.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

The bank's Short-Term IDR of 'B' is the only option mapping to a 'BB+' LT IDR.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The Short-Term IDR is sensitive to changes in the bank's LT IDR.

VR ADJUSTMENTS

The capitalisation and leverage score of 'bb+' is below the 'bbb' category implied score, due to the following adjustment reason: risk profile and business model (negative).

The funding and liquidity score of 'bb+' is below the 'bbb' category implied score, due to the following adjustment reason: deposit structure (negative).

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3'means ESG issues are credit neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Fell to 5-Year Low in September
data.description:
(13:31 GMT) *US Sep Trade Deficit $52.8B; Consensus Deficit $62.0B
(13:32 GMT) *Aug Trade Bal Revised To Deficit $59.3B From Deficit $59.55B
(13:32 GMT) *Aug Trade Bal Revised To Deficit $59.3B From Deficit $59.55B
(13:32 GMT) *Aug Trade Bal Revised To Deficit $59.3B From Deficit $59.55B
(13:34 GMT) *Sep Exports $289.3B, +3.0%; Imports $342.1B, +0.6%
(13:50 GMT) U.S. Trade Deficit Fell to 5-Year Low in September

By Matt Grossman

The U.S. trade deficit decreased in September, declining to the lowest level in five years as trade patterns adjust to the steep new tariffs levied by President Trump.

In September, American exports rose to $289.3 billion, while imports increased more modestly versus the August level to $342.1 billion. That yielded a trade deficit of $52.8 billion on the month, versus a $59.3 billion deficit in August.

The deficit hasn't been that slim since June 2020, when global supply chains were still recovering from the Covid-19 pandemic.

The three-month-old figures, published by the Commerce Department, are landing behind schedule as federal statistics agencies continue to catch up on data that couldn't be prepared for publication during the fall's government shutdown.

Stronger exports of gold and consumer goods, including pharmaceuticals, helped increase the sum of America's sales to foreigners. Imports grew more modestly, as smaller imports of computers and electronics helped offset an increase in the value of consumer products purchased from overseas.

data.title: Bangladesh president, feeling 'humiliated', wants to step down halfway through term
data.description:
Key points:
  • Shahabuddin wants to leave after February 12 election
  • President was elected unopposed in 2023 under now-ousted ruler
  • Shahabuddin says "my voice has been stifled"

By Krishna N. Das

Bangladeshi President Mohammed Shahabuddin said on Thursday he plans to step down midway through his term after February’s parliamentary election, telling Reuters he has felt humiliated by the interim government, led by Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus.

As head of state, Shahabuddin is commander-in-chief of the armed forces, but the role is largely ceremonial, and executive power rests with the prime minister and cabinet of the mainly Muslim country of 173 million people.

However, his position gained prominence when a student-led uprising forced long-time premier Sheikh Hasina to flee to New Delhi in August 2024, leaving him as the last remaining constitutional authority after parliament was dissolved.

Shahabuddin, 75, had been elected unopposed for a five-year term in 2023 as a nominee of Hasina’s Awami League party, which has been barred from contesting the February 12 election.

BANGLADESH PRESIDENT SAYS YUNUS SIDELINED HIM

“I am keen to leave. I am interested to go out,” he said in a WhatsApp interview from his official residence in Dhaka, in what he said was his first media interview since taking office.

“Until elections are held, I should continue,” Shahabuddin said. “I am upholding my position because of the constitutionally held presidency.”

The president said Yunus had not met him for nearly seven months, his press department had been taken away and, in September, his portraits were removed from Bangladeshi embassies around the world.

“There was the portrait of the president, picture of the president in all consulates, embassies and high commissions, and this has been eliminated suddenly in one night,” he said. “A wrong message goes to the people that perhaps the president is going to be eliminated. I felt very much humiliated.”

Shahabuddin said he had written to Yunus about the portraits, but no action was taken. "My voice has been stifled," he added.

Yunus’ press advisers did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

PRESIDENT IN CONTACT WITH ARMY CHIEF

The president said he was in regular touch with Army Chief General Waker-uz-Zaman, whose troops stood aside in August 2024 amid deadly protests against Hasina, sealing the fate of the veteran politician. Shahabuddin said Zaman had made it clear he had no intention of grabbing power.

Bangladesh has a history of military rule, but Zaman has said he wants democracy to return.

Shahabuddin said that, although some student protesters had initially demanded that he resign, no political party had asked him to do so in recent months.

Opinion polls suggest the Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) of former prime minister Khaleda Zia and the hardline Jamaat-e-Islami will be the frontrunners to form the next government. They were part of a coalition that ruled between 2001 and 2006.

Asked if Hasina, who had governed for 20 years, had tried to contact him after fleeing, Shahabuddin declined to answer. He said he had been independent since becoming president, not affiliated to any party.

data.title: Jobless Claims Rose Last Week But Remained Modest
data.description:
By Matt Grossman

The number of Americans filing new claims for unemployment benefits rose last week but stayed well within the range that has prevailed over the past year.

Last week, 236,000 initial jobless claims were filed, up from 192,000 the week before, the Labor Department said Thursday. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had forecast 223,000 new claims.

For almost all of 2025, the Labor Department has recorded between roughly 210,000 and 250,000 new claims each week, and the latest weekly figure suggests that December did not begin with any serious pickup in layoffs. Many economists suspect that the low late-November figure of 192,000 reflected an errant seasonal-adjustment factor due to the timing of Thanksgiving this year.

Continuing claims, which show the trend in the total size of the unemployed population, fell in the week through Nov. 29 to 1.84 million, versus 1.94 million a week earlier. The continuing-claims numbers lag the initial-claims tally by a week.

Mixed signals have divided economists' perspective on the job market's current state and future trajectory. On the surface, the latest monthly job figures look solid: September saw the addition of 119,000 new jobs and a slight uptick in the unemployment rate to a relatively modest 4.4%. Stability in the weekly jobless-claims numbers has also bolstered the case that the labor market is in solid shape.

Yet job creation has slowed markedly from recent years, likely a consequence both of cooling demand for workers and of slower growth in the number of people seeking jobs amid the immigration crackdown. A small share of people have faced layoffs, but those out of work face long and frustrating job searches amid sluggish hiring.

Concerns about the job market prompted the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates by a quarter percentage point at the central bank's final meeting of the year on Wednesday, easing monetary policy at a third straight meeting. Chair Jerome Powell said the reality in the labor market might be somewhat worse than it appears, given negative future revisions to labor-market statistics that many economists anticipate.

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

data.title: Deutsche Bank lifts J.B. Hunt rating to 'buy', hikes PT
data.description:
** Deutsche Bank upgrades rating on U.S. trucking firm J.B. Hunt Transport Services to "buy" from "hold",hikes PT to $227 from $133

** PT is an upside of 14.6% vs. stock's last close

** Brokerage says co stands to gain most from rising truck rates through 2026, encouraging more cost-efficient shifts to JBHT's rail and intermodal transport offerings

** Deutsche Bank expects co's earnings growth to outpace the market over the next two years

** 14 of 25 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 10 "hold" and 1 "sell"; median PT is $173 - data compiled by LSEG

** As of last close, shares up 16.1% YTD
